overall_summary: The Maryland PSC argues that FERC lacks jurisdiction over large load interconnection under the FPA, urging the Commission to defer to state authority and avoid asserting jurisdiction over end-use retail customers. It supports cost-causation-based upgrade assignment and co-location with new generation, while warning that curtailment incentives and partial generator suspensions could raise prices and harm reliability for existing ratepayers.

quotes.text: Achievement of the ANOPR's intended objective need not require the Commission to attempt to assert jurisdiction over entities and processes that otherwise fall under state jurisdiction.
quotes.concern: FERC jurisdiction over large load interconnection is unnecessary and should be avoided

quotes.text: The proposed ANOPR blurs this jurisdictional line by "asserting jurisdiction over the interconnection of large loads to the transmission system," where states have historically exercised their jurisdiction.
quotes.concern: ANOPR oversteps the federal-state boundary established by the FPA

quotes.text: large load customers are end-use customers that apply for interconnection service in order to purchase electricity at retail for their own consumption. They are not directly connected to the bulk power system–that is, their physical connection is to the low-voltage side of a substation, and their permitting and construction are strictly state and local matters.
quotes.concern: Large load facilities meet distribution-facility criteria under Order No. 888 and are subject to state, not federal, jurisdiction

quotes.text: The ANOPR premises the Commission's jurisdiction over large load interconnections on the analogy it makes to generator interconnections. This comparison is fundamentally flawed: a generator interconnection involves connecting a resource that injects electric power into the grid to meet the system's electrical demand. In contrast, large load interconnection involves connecting a facility that withdraws power from the grid.
quotes.concern: Generator interconnection analogy does not apply to load interconnection

quotes.text: the Maryland PSC cautions the Commission that states are likely to challenge whether FERC can properly exercise jurisdiction over the interconnection of large load. The Maryland PSC believes a jurisdictional dispute over load interconnection would be counterproductive to all parties' interests and should be avoided.
quotes.concern: Jurisdictional overreach risks litigation that would delay ANOPR objectives

quotes.text: the Maryland legislature has established that a "large load customer" is a commercial or industrial customer (for retail electric service) that "has or is projected to have an aggregate monthly demand of at least 100 MW…." Where the ANOPR would prescribe new standards and requirements on large load customers that exceed the 20 MW threshold—a much lower qualifying threshold—the Maryland PSC is concerned that if FERC were to exercise jurisdiction over load, these inconsistencies in these large load definitions would create confusion in the regulatory space
quotes.concern: Conflicting state and federal threshold definitions would cause regulatory confusion

quotes.text: Bringing new (large) load on simultaneously with a matching amount of new generation/storage would maintain the supply/demand balance existing on the grid, thus causing minimal impact to the marginal cost of energy and capacity and lessening the need for network upgrades.
quotes.concern: Co-location with new generation preserves market prices and reduces upgrade costs

quotes.text: netting load against generation supply at hybrid facilities could pose a risk to grid reliability. Depending on how the hybrid arrangement is configured and how it is treated by system planners, netting load against the generator's supply to the grid may risk the load: (a) escaping the "view" of grid operators during normal operations; and (b) being excluded from future planning scenarios.
quotes.concern: Netting load against generation hides it from operators and planners, creating reliability risk

quotes.text: Principle 4, as it purports to apply to load and hybrid facilities with load greater than 20 MW, would impose requirements and penalties directly on large load customers, which are retail customers currently subject to retail tariffs regardless of where they interconnect. This raises potential jurisdictional conflicts with state-level requirements placed on end-use customers via state-specific retail tariffs.
quotes.concern: Study deposits and penalties applied to retail load customers conflict with state tariff authority

quotes.text: the Commission should focus on solutions that would preclude speculation and hold the wholesale intermediary (over which FERC has jurisdiction) accountable for the accuracy of its load projections and supplying that load
quotes.concern: Anti-speculation rules should target LSEs under FERC jurisdiction, not retail customers

quotes.text: if a significant amount of curtailment is incentivized and implemented such that new large loads are added to the system faster than new generation can come online, a growing number of customers would be leaning on a relatively stagnant supply of electricity. This would mean that critical system conditions could be approached more frequently and reliability would be challenged more often.
quotes.concern: Curtailment incentives speed load growth without matching supply, worsening reliability

quotes.text: According to the IMM, removing 10,000 MW of nuclear generation in the entire PJM footprint would have increased capacity prices in the state of Maryland by approximately 47% ($526 million) relative to the 2025/2026 Base Residual Auction ("BRA") results.
quotes.concern: IMM data shows large load additions without new supply cause major capacity price increases in PJM

quotes.text: The Maryland PSC supports the principle of cost allocation on the basis of cost causation, and would emphasize that the total impact to grid reliability caused by a new large load or hybrid facility should be fully represented in the costs that they are assigned through interconnection studies.
quotes.concern: Full cost causation should drive upgrade cost assignment to large load customers

quotes.text: Existing generation currently serving customers should not be allowed to suspend their existing service, in whole or in part, simply to serve new large load. Such a suspension would have an adverse impact on cost and raise energy prices.
quotes.concern: Partial generator suspension to serve co-located load harms existing customers through higher prices

quotes.text: a path forward could be accomplished by requiring LSEs to identify the amount of large load additions they intend to supply, and to be held accountable for accommodating these additions in a manner that retains reliability and affordability for other customers. The LSE would need to demonstrate to the RTO/ISO and state regulators sufficient amounts of new generation or new demand response related to the amounts of new large loads to be served.
quotes.concern: LSE accountability for matched new supply is the recommended mechanism to protect existing customers

bins.name: State Authority Over Load Interconnection
bins.description: The Maryland PSC argues that large load interconnection falls under state, not federal, jurisdiction under the FPA. It contends the generator-interconnection analogy is flawed because large loads withdraw rather than inject power, their connections qualify as distribution facilities under Order No. 888, and FERC asserting jurisdiction risks litigation that would delay reform goals.

bins.name: Conflicting Threshold Definitions
bins.description: Maryland law defines large load customers at 100 MW or more, far above the ANOPR's 20 MW threshold. The Maryland PSC warns that overlapping federal and state definitions would create regulatory confusion and could extend FERC requirements to retail customers never intended to be covered.

bins.name: Joint Study With New Generation
bins.description: The Maryland PSC supports studying large load together with new generation and storage, finding that simultaneous addition of matched supply maintains market prices and minimizes the need for network upgrades.

bins.name: Deposits Targeted at Wholesale Intermediaries
bins.description: The Maryland PSC shares concern about speculative projects but opposes imposing deposit and penalty requirements directly on retail load customers. It recommends that anti-speculation obligations fall on load-serving entities, which are under FERC's wholesale jurisdiction.

bins.name: Netting Risk to Grid Visibility
bins.description: The Maryland PSC warns that netting large load against co-located generation could hide the load from grid operators and exclude it from planning scenarios, creating reliability risks that grow as large loads multiply.

bins.name: Curtailment Incentives Risk Reliability
bins.description: The Maryland PSC accepts that curtailment incentives may yield some system benefit but warns that accelerating load additions faster than new generation can come online will strain reliability and raise prices for all other customers.

bins.name: Full Reliability Costs in Studies
bins.description: The Maryland PSC supports cost-causation-based assignment of all reliability impacts through interconnection studies, and recommends that LSEs be required to demonstrate matched new supply before large load additions are approved.

bins.name: Protect Ratepayers From Cost Impacts
bins.description: The Maryland PSC uses PJM IMM data to show that adding large load without new supply can raise Maryland capacity prices by 47% and day-ahead energy prices by 28% to 115%. It supports full cost-causation assignment and opposes any framework that shifts those costs onto existing customers.

bins.name: New Generation Required for Co-location
bins.description: The Maryland PSC supports co-location only with new, not existing, generation. It opposes partial suspension of existing generators to serve co-located load, finding that approach raises prices without solving the underlying supply shortfall. It also flags netting as a reliability and planning blind spot.

bins.name: Curtailment Must Not Outpace New Supply
bins.description: The Maryland PSC opposes curtailment-based expediting if it allows load growth to outstrip supply additions, warning of more frequent critical system conditions and off-peak price increases for non-large-load customers.

bins.name: PJM Market and Capacity Concerns
bins.description: The Maryland PSC cites PJM IMM analysis showing large capacity and energy price increases from removing nuclear generation as a proxy for large load additions, and notes the 2026/2027 BRA cleared below the RTO Reliability Requirement, underscoring the tight supply outlook.

bins.name: Netted Load Hidden From Operators
bins.description: Netting co-located load against generation output can remove that load from real-time operator visibility and future planning models, a risk the Maryland PSC considers significant given the scale of anticipated large load growth.

bins.name: Load Growth Outstripping Supply
bins.description: The Maryland PSC warns that curtailment incentives and other tools that speed load additions without matching supply growth will increase the frequency of critical system conditions, and cites the 2024 northern Virginia data center trip event as a precedent for large-scale simultaneous load loss.

bins.name: Large Load Raises Capacity and Energy Prices
bins.description: Using PJM IMM analysis as evidence, the Maryland PSC documents that large load additions without offsetting new supply could increase Maryland capacity prices by roughly 47% and day-ahead energy prices by 28% to 115%.

bins.name: LSE Obligation for Matched New Supply
bins.description: The Maryland PSC recommends requiring LSEs to identify planned large load additions and demonstrate matched new generation or demand response before those loads interconnect, with penalties for LSEs that fail to meet supply obligations redistributed to affected customers.
